content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 307 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

August 19, 1977

A-77-08-007

Terry C. Andrus
Deputy County Counsel
Orange County
P.O. Box 1379
Santa Ana, CA 92702

Re: Opinion Request No. 77-020

Dear Mr. Andrus:

Thank you for your letter of July 27, 1977. You request an opinion concerning the reporting obligations that arise from the establishment of a legal defense fund on behalf of Ralph Diedrich, Orange County Supervisor. Mr. Diedrich was indicted on July 1, 1977, for alleged violations of the reporting requirements of the Political Reform Act. You ask whether monies donated to help defer Mr. Diedrich's legal expenses are reportable as "income" under Chapter 7 of the Act or as "campaign contributions" under Chapter 4. Because your question does not raise a substantial question under the Act, no opinion will be issued. However, I hope you find the following comments helpful.

The Commission has adopted a regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Section 18215, which defines a campaign contribution to include:

. . . any monetary or non-monetary payment for which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor that is received or made at the behest of:

(1) A candidate, unless it is clear from surrounding circumstances that the payment was received or made at his behest for personal purposes unrelated to his candidacy or status as an officeholder. . . .

2 Cal. Adm. Code §18215(b)

Terry C. Andrus
Page two
August 19, 1977


     Since the indictment of Mr. Diedrich relates to his
obligations as an officeholder, the standard set forth in the
regulation is met so long as Mr. Diedrich is considered a
"candidate." Government Code Section 82007 defines "candidate"
to mean:

     . . . an individual . . . who receives a contri-
bution or makes an expenditure or gives his con-
sent for any other person to receive a contribution or make an expenditure with a view to bring-
ing about his nomination or election to any elec-
tive office, whether or not the specific elective
office for which he will seek nomination or elec-
tion is known at the time the contribution is re-
ceived or the expenditure is made and whether or
not he has announced his candidacy or filed a
declaration of candidacy at such time.

     You indicate in your letter that "[o]n different
occasions, the Supervisor has publicly expressed an intent not
to run for reelection, as well as an intent to run under cer-
tain conditions. His present term expires in 1980." Since the
possibility exists that Mr. Diedrich will run again for elec-
tive office, and since, although he is apparently unsure, he
has not categorically ruled out that possibility, we would re-
gard him as a "candidate" within the meaning of Section 82007.
The fact that the outcome of his trial could influence voter
opinion on this question strengthens our view that money col-
lected for this purpose will be more political than personal
in nature.

     This interpretation of Section 82007 is consistent
with previous opinions of the Commission which have held that
left-over campaign funds which are used by an officeholder to
support political activities, such as traveling to speeches
and meetings and printing newsletters, retain their political
character and should be reported as Chapter 4 transactions
even though the election for which the funds were raised has
been concluded. See opinions requested by Janet Gray Hayes,
1 FPPC Ops. 210 (No. 75-145, Dec. 4, 1975) and Willie L.
Brown, Jr., 1 FPPC Ops. 67 (No. 75-055, July 2, 1975).

     The raising of money for a legal defense fund be-
cause of possible violations of the Political Reform Act by
a current officeholder seems similarly political in nature.
Accordingly, the appropriate place to disclose these transac-
tions would be on a campaign statement filed pursuant to
Chapter 4.

Terry C. Andrus
Page three
August 19, 1977


If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion
request, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18321. If you have any questions, please contact Michael J.
Baker, Chief of the Commission's Legal Division.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

OFFICES OF

THE COUNTY COUNSEL
County Of Orange

COUNTY ADMINISTRATION BUILDING • P. O. BOX 1379 • SANTA ANA, CA 92702 • 834-3300

July 27, 1977

Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California    95804

Re: Request for Opinion

Gentlemen:

ADRIAN KUYPER
County Counsel

WILLIAM J. McCOURT
Chief Assistant

ROBERT F. WHITMAN
ARTHUR C. WAHLSTEDT, JR.
Assistants

JOHN M. PATTERSON
JOHN W. ANDERSON
LAURENCE M. WATSON
VICTOR T. BELLEQUE
JOHN R. GRIST
DONALD G. SEVIER
WALTER D. WEBSTER
IRYNE C. BLACK
RALPH W. BENSON
KENNARD R. SMART, JR.
JAMES R. FLOUFNOY
TERRY C. ANDRUS
TERRY E. DIXON
EDWARD N. DURHAM
BARBARA E. THOMPSON
RICHARD D. OVIEDO
PAT MOORE
JULEE R. SAMS
NATIVIDAD F. CHAVIRA
BENJAMIN P. de MAYO
RONALD W. STENLAKE
R. DONALD McINTYRE
HOWARD SERBIN
Deputies

File No. P-297

On behalf of Ralph A. Diedrich, Orange County Supervisor, and pursuant to California Government Code Section 83114 and 2 California Administrative Code Section 18320, your opinion and advice is requested concerning Supervisor Diedrich's duties under the Political Reform Act of 1974. The facts are as follows:

Supervisor Diedrich and others were indicted by the Orange County Grand Jury on July 1, 1977, for alleged violations of the Political Reform Act. A number of individuals have expressed interest in creating a legal defense fund, wherein the monies raised would be used for the Supervisor's legal defense, and surplus funds, if any, would be returned to the donors on a pro rata basis.

On July 15, 1977, a "Committee Statement of Organization" Form 410 was filed with the Secretary of State for the above-referenced purpose (copy attached).

The question presented is whether monies received for this purpose are to be considered "income" reportable under Chapter 7 of the Act, or "campaign contributions" reportable under Chapter 4.

On different occasions, the Supervisor has publicly expressed an intent not to run for reelection, as well as an intent to run under certain conditions. His present term expires in 1980. Would that fact alter your analysis?

Thank you for your prompt assistance.

Very truly yours,

ADRIAN KUYPER, COUNTY COUNSEL

By ____________________
Terry C. Andrus, Deputy
